content:
# Leviticus Chapter 25 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 73r–73v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses on Mount Sinai: **2** "Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When you come into the land I am giving you, the land itself must observe a Sabbath rest — a Sabbath belonging to the LORD. **3** For six years you may sow your fields and prune your vineyards and gather in your crops, **4** but the seventh year is to be a complete Sabbath rest for the land — a Sabbath to the LORD. Do not sow your fields; do not prune your vineyards. **5** What grows on its own from last year's harvest, don't reap; the grapes that grow on your untended vines, don't pick. It is a year of full rest for the land. **6** But whatever the land produces during its Sabbath year, you and your servants and your hired hands and the foreigners staying with you may eat it — **7** and your livestock and the wild animals in your land as well. Everything the land yields may be eaten.

**8** "Count off seven Sabbath-years — seven times seven years — so that the seven Sabbath-years come to forty-nine years. **9** Then on the tenth day of the seventh month — the Day of Atonement — sound the ram's horn throughout all your land. **10** Consecrate the fiftieth year and proclaim liberty throughout the land for all its inhabitants. It shall be a Jubilee for you. Every one of you is to return to his own property; every one of you is to return to his own family. **11** The fiftieth year is to be a Jubilee for you — do not sow, do not reap what grows on its own, do not harvest the untended vines. **12** It is a Jubilee; it is to be holy for you. You may eat only what the fields produce on their own.

**13** "In this Year of Jubilee, everyone is to return to his own property. **14** If you sell land to your neighbor or buy land from him, do not take advantage of each other. **15** You are to buy from your neighbor according to the number of years since the last Jubilee, and he is to sell to you according to the number of remaining years of harvests. **16** When more years remain, raise the price; when fewer years remain, lower the price — because what he is really selling you is a number of harvests. **17** Do not take advantage of one another; fear your God. I am the LORD your God.

**18** "Follow my decrees and be careful to keep my laws, and you will live safely in the land. **19** Then the land will yield its fruit and you will eat your fill and live there in security. **20** You may wonder, 'What are we going to eat in the seventh year if we do not sow or harvest our crops?' **21** I will send my blessing on you in the sixth year so that the land will yield enough for three years. **22** While you plant during the eighth year, you will eat from the old harvest and will continue to eat from it until the harvest of the ninth year comes in.

**23** "The land must never be sold permanently, because the land is mine and you are only strangers and sojourners living here with me. **24** Throughout the land that you hold, you must provide for the land's redemption.

**25** "If your brother becomes poor and sells some of his property, his nearest kinsman-redeemer is to come and buy back what his brother sold. **26** If a man has no one to redeem it for him but he himself later prospers enough to buy it back, **27** he is to calculate the years since the sale and refund the difference to the man he sold it to; and so he returns to his own property. **28** If he cannot afford to get it back, what he sold will remain in the buyer's possession until the Year of Jubilee. It will be returned in the Jubilee, and he can then go back to his property.

**29** "If a man sells a house in a walled city, he retains the right to buy it back for a full year after its sale. **30** If it is not redeemed before a full year has passed, the house in the walled city becomes the permanent possession of the buyer and his descendants. It does not revert in the Jubilee. **31** But houses in villages without surrounding walls are to be treated like open fields — they can be redeemed, and they do revert in the Jubilee.

**32** "The Levites always retain the right to redeem their houses in the Levitical towns that they possess. **33** If a Levite redeems a house in a Levitical town — or if it is not redeemed — the house in a Levitical town reverts in the Jubilee, because the houses in Levitical towns are their holding among the Israelites. **34** But the pastureland belonging to their towns must never be sold; it is their permanent possession.

**35** "If your brother becomes poor and cannot support himself among you, help him as you would a foreigner or sojourner, so that he can continue living with you. **36** Do not charge him interest of any kind, but fear your God, and let your brother keep living with you. **37** Do not lend him money at interest, and do not sell him food for profit. **38** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan and to be your God.

**39** "If your brother becomes so poor that he sells himself to you, do not make him work as a slave. **40** Treat him as a hired hand or as a sojourner living with you. He is to work for you until the Year of Jubilee. **41** Then he and his children are to be released, and he will go back to his own family and return to the property of his ancestors. **42** Because they are my servants whom I brought out of Egypt, they are not to be sold as slaves. **43** Do not rule over them harshly; fear your God.

**44** "Your male and female slaves are to come from the nations around you — you may buy them from there. **45** You may also buy them from the foreigners residing among you and from their families living among you who were born in your land, and they will become your property. **46** You can will them to your children as inherited property and make them slaves permanently. But over your brothers, the Israelites, you must not rule over one another harshly.

**47** "If a foreigner or a sojourner living among you becomes wealthy and your brother living nearby becomes poor and sells himself to that foreigner or sojourner, or to a member of that foreigner's family, **48** after he has sold himself he retains the right of redemption. One of his brothers may redeem him, **49** or his uncle or his cousin or any close relative in his family may redeem him; or if he himself prospers, he may redeem himself. **50** He and his buyer are to count the years from when he sold himself up to the Year of Jubilee. The price of his release is to be based on the rate paid to a hired hand for that number of years. **51** If many years remain, he must repay a larger share of the price he was purchased for. **52** If only a few years remain, he is to calculate and repay accordingly. **53** He is to be treated as a hired hand year by year; his owner must not rule over him harshly in your sight.

**54** "Even if he is not redeemed in any of these ways, he and his children are to be released in the Year of Jubilee. **55** For the Israelites belong to me as servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of Egypt. I am the LORD your God."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### What Leviticus 25 Is Actually About

Leviticus 25 is arguably the most economically radical chapter in the entire Bible. It does not merely regulate commerce and labor; it systematically dismantles the mechanisms by which wealth permanently concentrates and poverty permanently compounds. The Sabbatical year (every seven years), the Jubilee (every fifty years), the laws on interest-free lending, the right of redemption, the restriction on permanent enslavement of fellow Israelites — all of these are not isolated rules but interlocking pieces of a single system designed to periodically reset the economic order and prevent any family from being locked out of the land forever.

The theological foundation of the whole system is stated explicitly and plainly in verse 23: "The land is mine, and you are only strangers and sojourners living here with me." And in verse 55: "The Israelites belong to me as servants." These two declarations answer the two great economic anxieties of the ancient world — the loss of land and the loss of freedom — with the same theological move: you cannot permanently lose what never finally belonged to you, and you cannot be permanently enslaved by a human master because you already belong to a divine one.

### Verse 10: The Jubilee — *Yovel*

"Proclaim liberty throughout the land for all its inhabitants" — this is the verse inscribed on the Liberty Bell in Philadelphia, and it is impossible to read the American history of that inscription without irony. The bell was cast in 1752 with this text from Leviticus 25:10. It was used to mark the birth of a republic that simultaneously held enslaved people and debated whether liberty was universal. The verse itself is about the liberation of enslaved Israelites and the return of forfeited land. Its placement on an instrument of national proclamation raises a question the founding generation could not quite face: whose liberty, and liberty for whom?

But the original text has its own precision. The Hebrew word *deror* — translated "liberty" or "release" — is a technical legal term. It appears in Jeremiah 34:8, 15, 17, where Zedekiah releases enslaved Israelites in a covenant ceremony and then retracts the release, and the LORD's response is devastating. It appears in Isaiah 61:1, where the Servant of the LORD is anointed to "proclaim release to the captives" — using *deror* — in the passage that Jesus reads in the Nazareth synagogue and says "Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing" (Luke 4:18–21). The Jubilee is not simply a legal mechanism for economic redistribution. In the prophetic imagination, it becomes the template for the eschatological liberation that God's Servant will announce: the year of the LORD's favor, the great Jubilee of history.

The word *yovel* — Jubilee — is related to the ram's horn (*shofar yovel*) blown on the Day of Atonement to inaugurate the year. The sound of liberation begins on the day of national repentance. This is not coincidental: the Jubilee is understood as flowing from the great Day of Atonement, from the act of divine forgiveness that precedes and enables the act of social restoration. You cannot truly release your neighbor until you have yourself been released from what you owe.

The fiftieth year completes a cycle of seven sevens (7 × 7 = 49) and adds one more year beyond it — the year that exceeds the Sabbath cycle, the year that does not merely rest the land but restores everything. It is a sabbath of sabbaths. In Jewish tradition, this structure anticipates the world to come: the age that exceeds history's Sabbath, the final rest into which all partial rests point.

### Verse 23: The Theological Foundation of All Property — "The Land Is Mine"

"The land must never be sold permanently, because the land is mine and you are only strangers and sojourners living here with me."

This verse is the linchpin of the chapter and one of the most important sentences in the Bible for any theology of economics, creation, and human relationship to the earth. Its logic is absolute: the land cannot be permanently transferred between human owners because human beings are not the ultimate owner. God is. All human landholding is therefore a stewardship, a tenancy, a sojourn — and never a final possession.

The words *gerim ve-toshavim* — "strangers and sojourners" — are remarkable in their context. These are the terms used throughout Leviticus and Numbers for the *foreigner* living among Israel: the non-Israelite who has attached himself to the covenant community. Israel is now placed in precisely the same legal category in relation to God. You are, with respect to me, what the resident alien is with respect to you: a temporary guest, dependent on my hospitality, living in a land you do not ultimately own. The ethical implications are immediate: how you treat the foreigner in your midst must be shaped by the recognition that you are a foreigner in God's midst.

This verse has had an enormous afterlife in Christian and Jewish social ethics. It underlies the prophetic condemnations of those who "add field to field" (Isaiah 5:8) — who treat land as permanently accumulate-able wealth. It underlies the Jubilee theology of the prophets. It underlies the theology of creation care: the earth is the LORD's (Psalm 24:1), and human beings are caretakers, not owners. In the New Testament, the earliest Jerusalem community's economic practice — "they had everything in common," "no one claimed that any of their possessions was their own" (Acts 4:32) — reads as a practical Jubilee, rooted in exactly this theological logic: if nothing is finally ours, then sharing everything costs nothing ultimate.

### Verses 25–34: The Go'el — Kinsman-Redeemer

The *go'el* is one of the most important and beautiful institutions in all of ancient Israel. The word is often translated "kinsman-redeemer," but the Hebrew root *ga'al* means something richer: to restore, to rescue, to buy back, to reclaim. The *go'el* is the family member whose obligation and privilege it is to step in when a relative has fallen into crisis — to buy back sold land, to redeem a relative from debt-slavery, to avenge a murdered kinsman, to marry a widow to preserve a dead brother's name and line.

The go'el system is the social safety net of ancient Israel, but it is explicitly a kinship safety net, rooted in the network of family loyalty (hesed). The laws of Leviticus 25 provide the legal framework; the book of Ruth provides the human face. Boaz is not just a generous landlord who lets Ruth glean in his fields. He is the go'el — or rather, he becomes the go'el when the closer kinsman refuses to act (Ruth 4). His willingness to redeem Elimelech's land and marry Ruth, at personal cost, is the living enactment of Leviticus 25:25: "If your brother becomes poor and sells some of his property, his nearest kinsman-redeemer is to come and buy back what his brother sold."

The theology of redemption in the New Testament draws explicitly on this imagery. God himself acts as the *go'el* of his people in Isaiah 41:14; 43:14; 44:6 — "your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel." The prophets describe the Exodus as an act of divine go'el-redemption, where God buys back his enslaved people at the cost of the plagues and the crossing of the sea. Paul describes redemption through Christ with the same vocabulary of purchase and release from bondage that governs Leviticus 25. "You were bought with a price" (1 Cor 6:20; 7:23) echoes the go'el logic: someone paid the price, bore the cost, restored what was lost.

### Verses 39–43: Israelites Cannot Be Permanently Enslaved

The logic here is as tight as a legal proof. When a fellow Israelite is forced by poverty to sell himself, he must not be treated as a permanent slave. He is to be treated as a hired hand. In the Jubilee he goes free with his children. The reason given is theological and non-negotiable: "They are my servants whom I brought out of Egypt" (v.42).

The Exodus is the founding act of Israel's identity, but it is also Israel's founding property transfer. When God liberated Israel from Egypt, he did not liberate them into autonomous self-ownership. He liberated them *into his own service*. They passed from Pharaoh's lordship to YHWH's lordship. This is why permanent enslavement of a fellow Israelite to another Israelite is a theological impossibility: you would be claiming to own someone who already belongs to God. The human master's claim is superseded by the divine master's prior claim.

This logic becomes foundational for Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 7:21–23: "You were bought with a price; do not become slaves of human masters." The Christian's freedom from anxiety about their social status — slave or free — rests on the same ground as the Israelite's Jubilee freedom: you belong to God, and no human claim of ownership can be ultimate.

The distinction in verses 44–46 — that foreign slaves may be held permanently while Israelite brothers may not — is among the most difficult passages in Leviticus for modern readers. It must be read within its ancient context: the chapter is not establishing chattel slavery as a universal good; it is regulating existing ancient practices while carving out a protected sphere for covenant community members. The prophetic trajectory (all human beings bear God's image; the foreigner is to be loved as yourself, Lev 19:34) bends away from this distinction over time. But the immediate logic of the chapter is not a defense of slavery; it is a systematic limitation of it, culminating in the declaration that all Israelites must be free.

### Verse 42: "They Are My Servants" — Theology of Belonging

"For the Israelites belong to me as servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of Egypt." This verse closes the chapter in the same way verse 23 anchors the land theology. Just as the land cannot be permanently sold because it belongs to God, so the people cannot be permanently enslaved because they belong to God. The two claims are structurally parallel: my land, my people.

The word *avadim* — servants, slaves — is used without apology. Israel's relationship to God is one of *avdut*: servitude, service, belonging-to. The Passover haggadah begins: *avadim hayinu le-Pharaoh be-Mitzrayim* — "We were slaves to Pharaoh in Egypt." The Exodus moved Israel from one *avdut* to another: from Pharaoh's corvée labor to YHWH's liturgical service. The difference is not the structure (servant/master) but the master. A master who sets you free every seven years, returns your land every fifty years, forbids oppression, and declares himself your God is a categorically different kind of lord than the one who works you to death building supply depots.

The chapter's closing verse — "I am the LORD your God" — is the same formula that opens the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:2). The Jubilee legislation and the Decalogue are held together by the same divine self-introduction: the God who brought you out of Egypt is the one who speaks. What he requires of you in your economic dealings with your neighbors flows from who he is and what he has done for you.

### The Architecture of the Chapter: Sevens, Fifties, and the Shape of Time

Leviticus 25 is built on the mathematics of Sabbath. The Sabbath day (every seven days) is extended to the Sabbath year (every seven years) and then to the Jubilee (every 7 × 7 + 1 = fifty years). This is not merely an interesting numerical pattern; it is a theological claim about the structure of time itself. History is not random or cyclical; it has a built-in rhythm of work and rest, accumulation and release, that is woven into its fabric by God.

The Jubilee falls on the year after the seventh Sabbath-year. It is the year that does not fit neatly into the seven-year cycle — the extra year, the year beyond the pattern, the year that disrupts and resets. In this sense it functions theologically as an anticipation of the eschaton: the event beyond history's regular rhythms, the great disruption that does not merely pause the cycle but ends it and begins something new. The prophets who use Jubilee language (Isaiah 61 most explicitly) understand it as a lens through which to interpret the coming of God's kingdom — not a new cycle beginning, but a Jubilee that does not end.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 111–112)

The Greek of Leviticus 25 in Vaticanus (pages 111–112) is well-preserved. The LXX translates *yovel* as *aphesis* — release, remission — rather than transliterating it. *Aphesis* is the same word used in the New Testament for the forgiveness of sins: "release," "remission," "cancellation of debt." When John the Baptist and Jesus announce *aphesis hamartiōn* — forgiveness of sins — they are using the Jubilee word. The theological resonance is not accidental: the forgiveness of sin is framed in the language of the Jubilee, the year of release, the cancellation of what is owed.

The LXX renders verse 10's *deror* as *aphesis* as well: "proclaim release (*aphesis*) throughout all the land." This makes the connection between Leviticus 25 and Isaiah 61 and Luke 4 lexically explicit in Greek: one and the same word covers the Jubilee release of land and slaves, the Servant's announcement in Isaiah, and Jesus's declaration in Nazareth. The Greek Bible ties these passages together with a single thread of vocabulary.

Verse 23 in the LXX: *hē gē ou prathēsetai eis bebaiōsin* — "the land shall not be sold for permanent confirmation/guarantee." The Greek *bebaiōsis* (firm establishment, legal guarantee) captures the legal force of the Hebrew *le-tzimitut*: a sale confirmed beyond recall, a transaction with no further right of redemption. God forbids the *bebaiōsis* — the irrevocable title transfer — of the land, because irrevocable human titles cannot stand against the divine title that precedes them all.

The go'el terminology in verses 25–34 is rendered in the LXX with *agorazō* (to buy/purchase) and *lytrōtēs* (redeemer) / *lytrousthai* (to redeem). These are the same roots (*lytron*, *lytrōsis*) that run through the New Testament's vocabulary of redemption: Christ as the one who pays the ransom (*lytron*) for many (Mark 10:45), who redeems us from the curse of the law (*exēgorasen*, Gal 3:13). The go'el of Leviticus 25 is in the background of every New Testament sentence that speaks of Christ's redeeming work.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 73r–73v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 111–112 (LXX).*
